title: Chapter 10: Structure Learning from Observational Data
text:
Key Concepts

Markov Equivalence and Observational Indistinguishability
Multiple causal structures (DAGs) can produce the same joint probability distribution. If two DAGs encode the same set of conditional independencies, no amount of observational data can distinguish them. They belong to the same Markov equivalence class, represented by a CPDAG (Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph) where directed edges indicate consensus on direction and undirected edges indicate ambiguity.


Structure Learning Algorithms
Two main approaches discover candidate DAGs from data. The constraint-based approach (PC algorithm) tests conditional independencies to build a skeleton, then orients edges using collider rules. The score-based approach (hill climbing with BIC) searches over DAG space to optimize a scoring function that balances fit and parsimony. Both assume causal sufficiency, faithfulness, and sufficient sample size.


Instrumental Variables (IV)
A variable Z that is correlated with the treatment (relevance), affects the outcome only through the treatment (exclusion), and is independent of confounders (independence). Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) uses the instrument to extract exogenous variation in the treatment, yielding an unbiased causal effect estimate even when confounders are unobserved.


Good Controls vs. Bad Controls
Not all control variables improve a regression estimate. Controlling for confounders (common causes of treatment and outcome) removes bias. Controlling for mediators (variables on the causal path) blocks the causal effect and underestimates the total effect. Controlling for colliders (common effects of treatment and outcome) opens spurious paths and creates bias. The DAG must guide variable selection.


The MNH Data-Driven Workflow
A five-step process: (1) learn the DAG skeleton from DHIS2 data using PC or hill-climbing algorithms, (2) orient ambiguous edges using expert knowledge and temporal ordering, (3) validate implied independencies against the data, (4) estimate causal effects using IV or adjusted regression, and (5) use the model for intervention decisions. This workflow integrates data-driven discovery with the expert-driven causal modeling of earlier chapters.

title: Chapter 10: Structure Learning from Observational Data
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter implements structure learning from simulated DHIS2 data, compares constraint-based and score-based algorithms, demonstrates instrumental variable estimation, and illustrates good-control versus bad-control pitfalls.

Simulating DHIS2-Like Data
We create a dataset with a known causal structure (8 variables reflecting facility staffing, equipment, training, drug availability, delivery patterns, and neonatal outcomes) so that we can evaluate how well structure learning algorithms recover the true DAG.

library(bnlearn)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(42)
n <- 3000  # facilities

# True causal structure (known to us, not the algorithm)
true_dag <- model2network(paste0(
  "[Training][DrugAvail][Equipment]",
  "[Staffing|Training]",
  "[CSection|Staffing:DrugAvail:Equipment]",
  "[Referral|Staffing:Equipment]",
  "[Volume|Staffing:Equipment]",
  "[NMR|CSection:Referral:Staffing:DrugAvail]"
))

# Simulate from known structure with realistic parameters
training    <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.6)
drug_avail  <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.7)
equipment   <- rnorm(n, 0.5, 0.15) |> pmin(1) |> pmax(0)
staffing    <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4 + 0.3 * training)
csection    <- 5 + 8 * staffing + 3 * drug_avail + 4 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
referral    <- 15 - 5 * staffing - 3 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 4)
volume      <- 50 + 30 * staffing + 20 * equipment + rnorm(n, 0, 15)
nmr         <- 35 - 8 * staffing - 4 * drug_avail - 3 * (csection > 10) -
               2 * (referral < 10) + rnorm(n, 0, 5)

dhis2 <- tibble(
  Training = factor(training, labels = c("No", "Yes")),
  DrugAvail = factor(drug_avail, labels = c("Stockout", "Available")),
  Equipment = equipment,
  Staffing = factor(staffing, labels = c("Low", "Adequate")),
  CSection = csection,
  Referral = referral,
  Volume = volume,
  NMR = nmr
)

head(dhis2)



PC Algorithm – Learning Structure from Data
The PC algorithm uses conditional independence tests to discover edges. It returns a CPDAG (partially directed graph) reflecting the Markov equivalence class that is consistent with the observed data.

# Constraint-based structure learning: PC algorithm (stable version)
# Uses conditional independence tests to discover edges

learned_pc <- pc.stable(dhis2, test = "mi-cg", alpha = 0.05)
# "mi-cg" = mutual information test for conditional Gaussian (mixed data)

# Compare learned vs. true structure
cat("True DAG edges:\n")
print(arcs(true_dag))

cat("\nLearned CPDAG edges:\n")
print(arcs(learned_pc))

# Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) — lower is better
shd_score <- shd(learned_pc, true_dag)
cat("\nStructural Hamming Distance:", shd_score, "\n")

# Which edges were correctly identified?
true_arcs <- arcs(true_dag)
learned_arcs <- arcs(learned_pc)
cat("\nTrue positives:", nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")
cat("False positives:", nrow(learned_arcs) - nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")
cat("False negatives:", nrow(true_arcs) - nrow(merge(true_arcs, learned_arcs)), "\n")



Score-Based Learning (Hill Climbing)
Hill climbing with BIC optimizes a score that balances fit and complexity. We compare the structures recovered by both approaches against the true DAG.

# Score-based structure learning: Hill Climbing with BIC
learned_hc <- hc(dhis2, score = "bic-cg")
# "bic-cg" = BIC for conditional Gaussian networks

# Compare all three structures
cat("=== Structure Comparison ===\n\n")

cat("PC algorithm edges:", nrow(arcs(learned_pc)), "\n")
cat("Hill climbing edges:", nrow(arcs(learned_hc)), "\n")
cat("True DAG edges:", nrow(arcs(true_dag)), "\n\n")

cat("SHD (PC vs. true):", shd(learned_pc, true_dag), "\n")
cat("SHD (HC vs. true):", shd(learned_hc, true_dag), "\n")

# Visualize all three
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
graphviz.plot(true_dag, main = "True DAG", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))
graphviz.plot(learned_pc, main = "PC Algorithm (CPDAG)", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))
graphviz.plot(learned_hc, main = "Hill Climbing (BIC)", shape = "ellipse",
              highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", fill = "#E24A3F", col = "#E24A3F"))



Instrumental Variable Estimation
We simulate an IV scenario where district wealth confounds the relationship between midwife density and NMR. Distance to the nearest training college serves as an instrument that affects midwife density but not NMR directly. Two-stage least squares (2SLS) recovers the true causal effect.

library(ivreg)  # for 2SLS estimation

# Does midwife density cause lower NMR?
# Instrument: distance to nearest training college

set.seed(123)
n_districts <- 500

# Simulate IV scenario
wealth        <- rnorm(n_districts, 50, 15)           # confounder
distance      <- runif(n_districts, 5, 200)            # instrument
midwife_density <- 20 - 0.05 * distance + 0.3 * wealth + rnorm(n_districts, 0, 3)
nmr_district  <- 40 - 0.4 * midwife_density - 0.2 * wealth + rnorm(n_districts, 0, 5)

iv_data <- tibble(
  NMR = nmr_district,
  Midwife = midwife_density,
  Distance = distance,
  Wealth = wealth
)

# Naive OLS (biased — omits wealth)
ols_naive <- lm(NMR ~ Midwife, data = iv_data)

# OLS with control (correct if we observe wealth)
ols_controlled <- lm(NMR ~ Midwife + Wealth, data = iv_data)

# 2SLS IV estimation (doesn't need wealth)
iv_est <- ivreg(NMR ~ Midwife | Distance, data = iv_data)

# Compare all three
cat("=== Causal Effect of Midwife Density on NMR ===\n")
cat("True effect:        -0.40\n")
cat("Naive OLS:         ", round(coef(ols_naive)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")
cat("Controlled OLS:    ", round(coef(ols_controlled)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")
cat("IV (2SLS):         ", round(coef(iv_est)["Midwife"], 3), "\n")

# First-stage F-statistic (test instrument strength)
first_stage <- lm(Midwife ~ Distance, data = iv_data)
cat("\nFirst-stage F-stat:", summary(first_stage)$fstatistic[1] |> round(1), "\n")
cat("(Rule of thumb: F > 10 for strong instrument)\n")



Good vs. Bad Controls in Regression
Two demonstrations: (1) controlling for a mediator underestimates the total effect of training on NMR, and (2) conditioning on a collider (quality rating) creates a spurious negative association between staffing and equipment.

# Demonstrate: controlling for a mediator UNDERESTIMATES the total effect
# Demonstrate: controlling for a collider CREATES spurious association

set.seed(456)
n <- 2000

# --- Mediator example ---
# True DAG: Training → Competency → NMR
training_m   <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
competency   <- 0.3 + 0.4 * training_m + rnorm(n, 0, 0.15)
nmr_m        <- 35 - 15 * competency + rnorm(n, 0, 4)

# Total effect of training (correct)
cat("=== Mediator Example ===\n")
cat("Total effect (no mediator control):",
    round(coef(lm(nmr_m ~ training_m))["training_m"], 2), "\n")
# Controlled direct effect (blocks causal path!)
cat("Direct effect (controlling mediator):",
    round(coef(lm(nmr_m ~ training_m + competency))["training_m"], 2), "\n")
cat("True total effect: -6.0\n\n")

# --- Collider example ---
# True DAG: Staffing → Quality ← Equipment; Staffing ⊥ Equipment
staffing_c   <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
equipment_c  <- rnorm(n, 0.5, 0.2)
quality      <- 0.3 * staffing_c + 0.4 * equipment_c + rnorm(n, 0, 0.1)

cat("=== Collider Example ===\n")
cat("Staffing-Equipment correlation (unconditional):",
    round(cor(staffing_c, equipment_c), 3), "\n")

# Condition on collider — creates spurious association
high_quality <- quality > median(quality)
cat("Staffing-Equipment correlation (among high-quality):",
    round(cor(staffing_c[high_quality], equipment_c[high_quality]), 3), "\n")
cat("Expected: ~0 unconditional, negative conditional (collider bias)\n")

# Visualize collider bias
tibble(Staffing = staffing_c, Equipment = equipment_c,
       Quality = ifelse(quality > median(quality), "High", "Low")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(Staffing), y = Equipment, fill = Quality)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("High" = "#83BD00", "Low" = "#E24A3F")) +
  labs(title = "Collider Bias: Conditioning on Quality of Care",
       subtitle = "Among high-quality facilities, staffing and equipment appear negatively correlated",
       x = "Staffing (0 = Low, 1 = Adequate)", y = "Equipment Index") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: Chapter 10: Structure Learning from Observational Data
text:
Key Takeaways

Data alone cannot determine causation. Markov equivalence means multiple DAGs fit the same data. Domain knowledge is essential to resolve ambiguity.
Structure learning discovers candidate DAGs. The PC algorithm (constraint-based) and hill climbing (score-based) recover the skeleton; colliders are the key to orientation.
Instrumental variables overcome confounding. When you cannot control for confounders, a valid instrument recovers the causal effect via 2SLS.
Not all controls are good controls. Controlling for mediators underestimates effects; controlling for colliders creates bias. Let the DAG guide your regression specification.

This chapter closes the loop on the complete Inference and Intervention toolkit:







Chapter
Tool

Qualitative DAGs


4–5
Bayesian networks and inference


6
Causal reasoning pitfalls (Simpson’s Paradox, Prosecutor’s Fallacy)


7
Decision analysis and do-calculus


8
Resource allocation under uncertainty


9
Multi-agent game theory


10
Data-driven discovery



The MNH program analyst’s workflow brings all these pieces together: build a DAG from expert interviews (Chapters 2–3), quantify it with DHIS2 data (Chapters 4–5, 10), avoid causal reasoning traps (Chapter 6), make allocation decisions (Chapters 7–8), anticipate strategic responses (Chapter 9), and validate and refine with new data (Chapter 10).
Causal models are not just academic abstractions – they are practical tools for answering the questions that matter most in global health: Where should we invest? What should we do? How do we know it will work?

title: Chapter 8: Multi-Country Resource Allocation
text:
Key Concepts

Multi-Country Resource Allocation
A large-scale MNH program must allocate pooled donor funding across multiple countries, each with different baseline mortality, health system capacity, and government readiness. The optimal allocation depends not just on where lives can be saved most efficiently, but on how uncertain we are about each country’s trajectory.


Portfolio Diversification
Borrowing from financial portfolio theory, a tiered allocation strategy that weights by burden and capacity score (with floors and ceilings per country) can reduce the risk of catastrophic shortfall. Concentrating funds in the highest-burden country maximizes expected value but carries the most variance; spreading funds equally lowers both expected value and risk.


Sequential Decision Making
The program does not have to commit all funds on day one. A phased approach (commit seed funding, observe pilot results, then scale or reallocate) enables better-informed decisions. Backward induction solves these multi-stage problems by starting at the terminal outcomes and working backward through each decision point.


The Cost of Delay
Sequential decision making is valuable but not free. Every month of delay means preventable deaths that could have been averted. The program must balance the information value of waiting against the human cost of inaction – a distinctive feature of public health versus commercial allocation problems.


Crowding Out vs. Crowding In
When the program invests in a country, other donors and the government may reduce their own spending (crowding out) or increase it (crowding in). On average, these effects may roughly cancel, but the variance matters: in some cases the program’s dollar buys only $0.70 of net new investment.

title: Chapter 8: Multi-Country Resource Allocation
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter implements Monte Carlo simulation for comparing multi-country allocation strategies, grid search for optimal allocation, and sensitivity analysis of co-financing uncertainty.

Monte Carlo Simulation Setup
We define country parameters (target lives, effectiveness per dollar, uncertainty) and a simulation function that draws government co-financing scenarios and computes total lives saved for a given allocation vector.

set.seed(42)
n_sims <- 10000

# Country parameters: target lives, expected lives per $M, sd per $M
countries <- data.frame(
  country = c("Country A", "Country C", "Country B"),
  target = c(45000, 27000, 38000),
  lives_per_M = c(610, 356, 462),
  sd_per_M = c(185, 95, 130),  # uncertainty in effectiveness
  p_high = c(0.40, 0.50, 0.45)  # P(high gov co-financing)
)

# Simulate outcomes for a given allocation (as proportions of budget)
simulate_outcomes <- function(alloc, countries, n_sims) {
  total_lives <- numeric(n_sims)
  for (i in 1:n_sims) {
    lives <- 0
    for (j in 1:nrow(countries)) {
      # Government co-financing scenario
      gov <- sample(c("High", "Med", "Low"), 1,
                    prob = c(countries$p_high[j],
                             1 - countries$p_high[j] - 0.10,
                             0.10))
      multiplier <- switch(gov, High = 1.0, Med = 0.6, Low = 0.25)
      # Lives saved = allocation * lives per $M * multiplier + noise
      lives_j <- alloc[j] * rnorm(1, countries$lives_per_M[j],
                                      countries$sd_per_M[j]) * multiplier
      lives <- lives + max(0, lives_j)
    }
    total_lives[i] <- lives
  }
  total_lives
}

# Three strategies (as relative allocation units, not dollar amounts)
strat_concentrate <- c(70, 15, 15)   # Heavy Country A
strat_spread      <- c(33, 33, 33)   # Equal
strat_tiered      <- c(45, 23, 32)   # Weighted



Visualizing Strategy Outcomes
A violin plot with embedded box plot shows the full distribution of simulated outcomes for each allocation strategy across 10,000 Monte Carlo draws.

library(ggplot2)

# Run simulations
sim_conc   <- simulate_outcomes(strat_concentrate, countries, n_sims)
sim_spread <- simulate_outcomes(strat_spread, countries, n_sims)
sim_tiered <- simulate_outcomes(strat_tiered, countries, n_sims)

# Combine into data frame
sim_df <- data.frame(
  lives = c(sim_conc, sim_spread, sim_tiered),
  strategy = rep(c("Concentrate", "Spread", "Tiered"),
                 each = n_sims)
)

# Violin plot with embedded box plot
ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = strategy, y = lives / 1000, fill = strategy)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6, draw_quantiles = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.8, outlier.shape = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "Allocation Strategy",
       y = "Total Lives Saved (thousands)",
       title = "Simulated Outcomes: 3-Country Allocation",
       subtitle = "10,000 Monte Carlo draws | Violin shows full distribution") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")



Optimal Allocation via Grid Search
A grid search over the allocation space (in 5% increments, subject to the constraint that allocations sum to 100%) identifies the best allocation by expected value and by worst-case (maximin) criterion.

# Grid search over allocation space
# Constraint: a_A + a_C + a_B = 100 (percentage units)
grid_step <- 5  # 5% increments
results <- data.frame()

for (a_A in seq(0, 100, grid_step)) {
  for (a_C in seq(0, 100 - a_A, grid_step)) {
    a_B <- 100 - a_A - a_C
    alloc <- c(a_A, a_C, a_B)

    # Quick simulation (fewer draws for speed)
    sim <- simulate_outcomes(alloc, countries, n_sims = 2000)

    results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
      a_A = a_A, a_C = a_C, a_B = a_B,
      ev = mean(sim),
      sd = sd(sim),
      p5 = quantile(sim, 0.05)  # 5th percentile (worst case)
    ))
  }
}

# Best by expected value
best_ev <- results[which.max(results$ev), ]
cat("Max EV allocation:", best_ev$a_A, "/",
    best_ev$a_C, "/", best_ev$a_B, "\n")
cat("Expected lives:", round(best_ev$ev), "\n")

# Best by worst-case (maximin)
best_p5 <- results[which.max(results$p5), ]
cat("\nMaximin allocation:", best_p5$a_A, "/",
    best_p5$a_C, "/", best_p5$a_B, "\n")
cat("5th percentile lives:", round(best_p5$p5), "\n")



Sensitivity Analysis
This plot shows how varying Country A’s probability of high government co-financing affects the expected lives saved for each strategy. The crossover point identifies the threshold at which concentrating funds in Country A becomes optimal.

library(tidyr)

# Sensitivity: vary Country A P(High co-financing) from 0.1 to 0.8
p_seq <- seq(0.1, 0.8, by = 0.05)
sens_results <- data.frame()

for (p_high_A in p_seq) {
  # Update Country A's co-financing probability
  countries_mod <- countries
  countries_mod$p_high[1] <- p_high_A

  ev_conc <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_concentrate, countries_mod, 3000))
  ev_spr  <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_spread, countries_mod, 3000))
  ev_tier <- mean(simulate_outcomes(strat_tiered, countries_mod, 3000))

  sens_results <- rbind(sens_results, data.frame(
    p_high = p_high_A,
    Concentrate = ev_conc,
    Spread = ev_spr,
    Tiered = ev_tier
  ))
}

# Pivot to long format and plot
sens_long <- pivot_longer(sens_results, cols = -p_high,
                          names_to = "Strategy", values_to = "EV")

ggplot(sens_long, aes(x = p_high, y = EV / 1000, color = Strategy)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.40, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "P(High Gov't Co-financing) for Country A",
       y = "Expected Lives Saved (thousands)",
       title = "Sensitivity: How Country A's Co-financing Risk Affects Strategy Choice",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = current estimate (p = 0.40)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: Chapter 8: Multi-Country Resource Allocation
text:
Key Takeaways

Resource allocation under uncertainty requires balancing expected impact against risk. The optimal allocation depends not just on where lives can be saved most efficiently, but on how uncertain we are about each country’s trajectory.
Portfolio thinking applies to public health: diversification reduces catastrophic shortfall risk. Sequential decisions (pilot then scale) add value by resolving uncertainty, but delay costs lives.
Government co-financing is the dominant uncertainty; small shifts in the probability of high co-financing change the optimal strategy. Monte Carlo simulation in R makes it possible to quantify confidence intervals and compare strategies rigorously.

title: Chapter 8: Multi-Country Resource Allocation
text:
Looking Ahead
Next in Chapter 9 – Game Theory and Strategic Interactions, we move from treating stakeholders as uncertain variables to modeling them as strategic agents:

Nash equilibrium in donor coordination games
Commitment devices that bind government co-financing
Mechanism design for aligning incentives across funders
MNH application: Designing a co-financing framework that maximizes collective impact

Chapter 8 solved the allocation problem as if government and donor behavior were exogenous uncertainties. Chapter 9 recognizes that these actors have their own objectives – and designs strategies that account for their rational self-interest.

title: Chapter 6: Simpson’s Paradox & Cost-Effectiveness
text:
Key Concepts

Simpson’s Paradox
A trend that appears in aggregated data reverses or disappears when the data is disaggregated by a confounding variable. The paradox arises when a lurking variable is associated with both the treatment and the outcome. Decisions based on aggregated data can be exactly wrong; resolution requires causal reasoning, not just statistical adjustment.


Cost Per Life Saved (CPLS)
The total program expenditure divided by the number of additional lives saved attributable to the intervention. Lower CPLS means greater efficiency. Naive comparisons of CPLS across countries can be deeply misleading because baseline severity, health system capacity, and population density confound the treatment-outcome relationship.


The Prosecutor’s Fallacy
Confusing \(P(\text{Evidence} \mid \text{Hypothesis})\) with \(P(\text{Hypothesis} \mid \text{Evidence})\). The probability of observing the evidence given that a hypothesis is true is not the same as the probability that the hypothesis is true given the evidence. Bayes’ rule and the base rate are needed to convert one into the other.


Confounders vs. Mediators
Always condition on confounders (common causes of treatment and outcome) to remove spurious associations. Never condition on mediators (variables on the causal path between treatment and outcome), because doing so blocks the very causal effect you are trying to estimate. Getting this distinction wrong leads to Simpson’s Paradox or underestimation of treatment effects.


Confounder-Adjusted Rankings
Naive ranking of countries by raw CPLS concentrates investment in countries with strong health systems and low mortality burden. Causal ranking, which adjusts for baseline NMR, health system capacity, population size, and existing coverage, targets countries where the causal effect of intervention is greatest. The causal approach can save thousands of additional lives with the same budget.

title: Chapter 6: Simpson’s Paradox & Cost-Effectiveness
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter uses R to simulate and analyze Simpson’s Paradox and the Prosecutor’s Fallacy, demonstrating how aggregated data can mislead and how Bayes’ rule corrects diagnostic reasoning.

Simulating Simpson’s Paradox
We begin by generating an MNH dataset where a confounder (country group) drives both treatment assignment and baseline risk, producing a Simpson’s Paradox in the aggregated data.

library(tidyverse)

set.seed(42)

# Simulate MNH data exhibiting Simpson's Paradox
# Confounder: country_group (high-burden vs low-burden)
n <- 2000

mnh_data <- tibble(
  # Confounder: country group determines both treatment and baseline risk
 country_group = sample(c("High-burden", "Low-burden"), n, replace = TRUE,
                         prob = c(0.6, 0.4)),
  # Treatment assignment depends on country group (confounder)
  cpap = case_when(
    country_group == "High-burden" ~ rbinom(n, 1, 0.75),
    country_group == "Low-burden"  ~ rbinom(n, 1, 0.25)
  ),
  # Outcome depends on BOTH country group and treatment
  # CPAP has a positive causal effect (+10pp), but high-burden has lower baseline
  survival_prob = case_when(
    country_group == "High-burden" & cpap == 1 ~ 0.68,
    country_group == "High-burden" & cpap == 0 ~ 0.61,
    country_group == "Low-burden"  & cpap == 1 ~ 0.85,
    country_group == "Low-burden"  & cpap == 0 ~ 0.82
  ),
  survived = rbinom(n, 1, survival_prob)
)

# Aggregated result (misleading)
mnh_data |>
  group_by(cpap) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(survived), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

# Disaggregated result (correct)
mnh_data |>
  group_by(country_group, cpap) |>
  summarise(survival_rate = mean(survived), n = n(), .groups = "drop")



Visualizing Aggregated vs. Stratified Results
The side-by-side comparison shows how the same data yields opposite conclusions depending on whether you stratify by the confounder.

library(ggplot2)

# Prepare labels
mnh_data <- mnh_data |>
  mutate(cpap_label = ifelse(cpap == 1, "CPAP", "No CPAP"))

# Aggregated view (misleading)
p_agg <- ggplot(mnh_data, aes(x = cpap_label, y = survived, fill = cpap_label)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "bar", width = 0.6) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot, geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CPAP" = "#005CB9", "No CPAP" = "#FA7650")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Aggregated: CPAP Looks Harmful",
       y = "Survival Rate", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Stratified view (correct)
p_strat <- ggplot(mnh_data, aes(x = cpap_label, y = survived, fill = cpap_label)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "bar", width = 0.6) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot, geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ country_group) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CPAP" = "#005CB9", "No CPAP" = "#FA7650")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Stratified: CPAP Helps in BOTH Groups",
       y = "Survival Rate", x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

library(patchwork)
p_agg + p_strat +
  plot_annotation(title = "Simpson's Paradox in MNH Data",
                  subtitle = "Same data, opposite conclusions")



Naive vs. Confounder-Adjusted Regression
The naive regression omits the confounder and produces a negative (misleading) CPAP coefficient. The adjusted regression includes the confounder and correctly estimates a positive causal effect.

# Naive regression: ignores confounder
naive_model <- lm(survived ~ cpap, data = mnh_data)
summary(naive_model)
# Expected: CPAP coefficient is NEGATIVE (misleading)

# Confounder-adjusted regression: includes country_group
adjusted_model <- lm(survived ~ cpap + country_group, data = mnh_data)
summary(adjusted_model)
# Expected: CPAP coefficient is POSITIVE (correct)

# Compare coefficients side by side
tibble(
  Model = c("Naive (no confounder)", "Adjusted (with confounder)"),
  CPAP_Coefficient = c(coef(naive_model)["cpap"],
                       coef(adjusted_model)["cpap"]),
  Interpretation = c("CPAP appears harmful",
                     "CPAP is beneficial (+7pp)")
)



Bayesian Network Encoding the Causal Structure
A Bayesian network that encodes the fork (confounder) structure automatically handles Simpson’s Paradox in its queries.

library(bnlearn)

# Prepare data for bnlearn (factors required)
bn_data <- mnh_data |>
  mutate(
    country_group = factor(country_group),
    cpap = factor(cpap, labels = c("No", "Yes")),
    survived = factor(survived, labels = c("Died", "Survived"))
  ) |>
  select(country_group, cpap, survived) |>
  as.data.frame()

# Define the causal structure (DAG)
dag <- model2network("[country_group][cpap|country_group][survived|cpap:country_group]")
plot(dag)

# Fit the BN with maximum likelihood estimates
fitted_bn <- bn.fit(dag, bn_data)

# Query: P(survived | do(cpap = Yes)) using causal reasoning
# Marginalizing over country_group (the back-door adjustment)
cpquery(fitted_bn,
        event = (survived == "Survived"),
        evidence = (cpap == "Yes"),
        n = 100000)

# Compare: P(survived | cpap = Yes, country_group = High-burden)
cpquery(fitted_bn,
        event = (survived == "Survived"),
        evidence = (cpap == "Yes" & country_group == "High-burden"),
        n = 100000)

# The BN correctly handles the conditioning automatically



Prosecutor’s Fallacy Calculator
This Bayes’ rule calculator demonstrates how the positive predictive value (PPV) of a diagnostic test depends critically on the base rate (prevalence), not just on sensitivity and specificity.

# Bayes' rule calculator for diagnostic tests
bayes_diagnostic <- function(sensitivity, specificity, prevalence) {
  # P(positive | disease) = sensitivity
  # P(negative | no disease) = specificity
  # P(positive | no disease) = 1 - specificity (false positive rate)
  # P(disease) = prevalence

  p_pos_given_disease    <- sensitivity
  p_pos_given_no_disease <- 1 - specificity
  p_disease              <- prevalence
  p_no_disease           <- 1 - prevalence

  # Total probability of a positive test
  p_positive <- (p_pos_given_disease * p_disease) +
                (p_pos_given_no_disease * p_no_disease)

  # Bayes' rule: P(disease | positive)
  p_disease_given_pos <- (p_pos_given_disease * p_disease) / p_positive

  # Return results
  tibble(
    Metric = c("Sensitivity", "Specificity", "Prevalence",
               "P(Positive Test)", "P(Disease | Positive) [PPV]",
               "Prosecutor's Fallacy Would Claim"),
    Value = c(
      scales::percent(sensitivity, accuracy = 0.1),
      scales::percent(specificity, accuracy = 0.1),
      scales::percent(prevalence, accuracy = 0.1),
      scales::percent(p_positive, accuracy = 0.1),
      scales::percent(p_disease_given_pos, accuracy = 0.1),
      scales::percent(sensitivity, accuracy = 0.1)
    )
  )
}

# Preeclampsia screening example
bayes_diagnostic(
  sensitivity = 0.90,
  specificity = 0.85,
  prevalence  = 0.05
)

# What if prevalence is higher (e.g., high-risk population)?
bayes_diagnostic(
  sensitivity = 0.90,
  specificity = 0.85,
  prevalence  = 0.20
)

At 5% prevalence, the PPV is approximately 24%. At 20% prevalence, the PPV rises to approximately 60%. The same test with the same sensitivity and specificity, but the meaning of a positive result depends entirely on the base rate.

title: Chapter 6: Simpson’s Paradox & Cost-Effectiveness
text:
Key Takeaways

Simpson’s Paradox arises when a confounder creates a fork structure in the DAG. Aggregated data can reverse the true causal effect. Always stratify by confounders, never by mediators.
The Prosecutor’s Fallacy confuses \(P(E \mid H)\) with \(P(H \mid E)\). Base rates matter. Use Bayes’ rule to compute the correct posterior probability.
In MNH cost-effectiveness, naive cross-country comparisons mislead because baseline severity and health system strength confound the treatment-outcome relationship. Causal adjustment can change which countries and interventions are prioritized – and save thousands of additional lives.

title: Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models

After this chapter, you will be able to:

Define probability distributions over discrete variable states and apply the basic axioms
Compute conditional probabilities and construct Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)
Apply Bayes’ rule to update beliefs when new evidence arrives
Explain the Causal Markov Condition and how a DAG factorizes a joint distribution
Build a quantitative Bayesian network in R using bnlearn and query it for MNH policy decisions

title: Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models
text:
Key Concepts

Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)
A CPT lists P(Child | Parents) for every combination of parent and child states. Each row in the table defines a conditional distribution, and every row must sum to 1. CPTs are the quantitative building blocks of a Bayesian network – they encode the strength of causal relationships that qualitative models could only describe with [+] and [-] signs. In practice, CPTs are estimated from facility-level data, expert elicitation, or both.


Bayes’ Rule
Bayes’ rule lets us “flip” the conditioning direction: given P(Evidence | Cause), it computes P(Cause | Evidence). The formula is P(A | B) = P(B | A) x P(A) / P(B). The prior P(A) represents what we believed before seeing evidence, the likelihood P(B | A) represents how probable the evidence is if the hypothesis is true, and the posterior P(A | B) represents our updated belief after incorporating evidence. This is the fundamental operation of situational assessment – reasoning backward from what we observe to what is likely true.


The Causal Markov Condition
Each variable in a DAG is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. This means the full joint distribution factorizes as a product of CPTs: P(X1, X2, …, Xn) = Product of P(Xi | Parents(Xi)). This factorization reduces computational complexity from exponential to manageable – instead of specifying millions of probabilities, the causal structure lets us specify just a few hundred.


Bayesian Network
A Bayesian network equals a DAG plus a CPT for every node. It allows us to compute exact probabilities for any query, update beliefs with Bayes’ rule when evidence arrives, and compare interventions using the do-operator. The do-operator severs incoming arrows to the intervention variable and sets it to a fixed value, giving us the causal effect rather than a mere correlation.

title: Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter builds a complete Bayesian network in R using bnlearn, specifies CPTs for each node, and demonstrates forward queries, interventional queries, and sensitivity analysis.

Define the Network Structure
We use bnlearn’s model string syntax to specify the DAG. The notation [Node|Parent1:Parent2] means a node is conditional on its parents, while [Node] alone denotes a root node with no parents.

library(bnlearn)

# Define the DAG structure using bnlearn's model string syntax
# [Node] means no parents; [Node|Parent1:Parent2] means conditional on parents
dag <- model2network(
  "[Staffing][Equipment][Quality|Staffing:Equipment][NMR|Quality]"
)

# Inspect the structure
dag
nodes(dag)    # "Staffing" "Equipment" "Quality" "NMR"
arcs(dag)     # Shows the 3 directed edges



Specify CPTs
Each CPT is defined as an array with named dimensions. Root nodes get simple marginal distributions, while child nodes get conditional distributions indexed by all parent state combinations.

# Define CPTs using arrays (states must match exactly)
cpt_staffing <- array(c(0.40, 0.60), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Staffing = c("Adequate", "Low")))

cpt_equipment <- array(c(0.35, 0.65), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_quality <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Adequate + Available
    0.50, 0.50,  # Low + Available
    0.55, 0.45,  # Adequate + Unavailable
    0.20, 0.80), # Low + Unavailable
  dim = c(2, 2, 2),
  dimnames = list(
    Quality   = c("Good", "Poor"),
    Staffing  = c("Adequate", "Low"),
    Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_nmr <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Good quality
    0.40, 0.60), # Poor quality
  dim = c(2, 2),
  dimnames = list(NMR = c("Low", "High"), Quality = c("Good", "Poor")))

# Combine into a fitted BN
bn_mnh <- custom.fit(dag, list(
  Staffing = cpt_staffing, Equipment = cpt_equipment,
  Quality = cpt_quality, NMR = cpt_nmr))



Query the Network
The cpquery() function uses likelihood weighting to estimate conditional probabilities from the network. For exact inference, we convert the network to a junction tree using the gRain package.

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Low)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Low"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.44

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Adequate, Equipment = Available)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Adequate" & Equipment == "Available"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.22

# Exact inference using grain (junction tree)
library(gRain)
bn_grain <- as.grain(bn_mnh)
querygrain(bn_grain, nodes = "NMR")
# Returns marginal P(NMR) across all facilities



Visualize the Network
Two visualization options are available: bnlearn’s built-in plot using Rgraphviz, and ggdag for publication-quality figures built on ggplot2.

# Option 1: bnlearn's built-in plot (uses Rgraphviz)
graphviz.plot(bn_mnh, main = "MNH Bayesian Network",
  shape = "rectangle",
  highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", col = "#E24A3F", fill = "#E24A3F"))

# Option 2: ggdag for publication-quality figures
library(ggdag)
library(dagitty)

mnh_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Staffing [pos="0,0"]
  Equipment [pos="2,0"]
  Quality [pos="1,1"]
  NMR [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
}')

ggdag(mnh_dag) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Causal Model (4 Nodes)")



Interventional Queries
The mutilated() function implements the do-operator – it severs incoming arrows to the intervention variable and sets it to a fixed value. This lets us compare the causal effect of different interventions side by side.

# Simulate do(Staffing = Adequate) by mutilating the network
bn_do_adequate <- mutilated(bn_mnh, evidence = list(Staffing = "Adequate"))

# P(NMR | do(Staffing = Adequate))
querygrain(as.grain(bn_do_adequate), nodes = "NMR")

# Compare scenarios
scenarios <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Status Quo", "do(Staff=Adequate)",
               "do(Equip=Available)", "do(Both)"),
  P_NMR_High = c(
    querygrain(as.grain(bn_mnh),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate",
                              Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"]))

# Display comparison
scenarios$Reduction <- scenarios$P_NMR_High[1] - scenarios$P_NMR_High
print(scenarios)

title: Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models
text:
Key Takeaways

Quantitative causal models = DAG + CPTs = Bayesian Network. They allow us to compute exact probabilities, update beliefs with Bayes’ rule, and compare interventions using the do-operator.
Probability fundamentals: States must be MECE; probabilities must sum to 1. CPTs encode the quantitative relationships in the DAG.
Bayes’ rule lets us reason backward from evidence to causes – essential for situational assessment.
The Causal Markov Condition simplifies computation from exponential to manageable. Sensitivity analysis reveals which interventions have the largest expected impact – in the MNH model, staffing has a larger marginal effect than equipment, but the two are synergistic.

title: Chapter 2: Qualitative Causal Models
text:
Key Concepts

The Four Node Types
A qualitative causal model uses four types of nodes: Probabilistic nodes (ovals) represent variables with uncertain states determined by nature. Objective nodes (hexagons) represent goals or performance metrics the decision-maker wants to optimize – they are always leaf nodes. Strategic option nodes (rectangles) represent deliberate choices made by a decision-maker – they are root nodes. Function nodes (chevrons) represent variables whose state is deterministically determined by their parents with no residual uncertainty.


Signed Directed Links
An arrow from node A to node B means that A is a direct cause of B – changing A can change B while holding all other parents of B fixed. The sign [+] indicates that increasing A increases B, while [-] indicates that increasing A decreases B. The overall sign of a directed path is the product of the individual link signs, where [+] x [+] = [+], [-] x [-] = [+], and [+] x [-] = [-].


The Three Triplet Structures
The three fundamental triplet structures govern all information flow in a DAG. In a serial triplet (chain, A -> B -> C), B is a mediator and conditioning on B blocks the path. In a diverging triplet (fork, A <- B -> C), B is a common cause and conditioning on B removes the spurious correlation. In a converging triplet (collider, A -> B <- C), the path is naturally blocked but conditioning on B opens it, creating collider bias through the “explaining away” effect.


d-Separation
Two nodes X and Y are d-separated given a conditioning set Z if every path between them is blocked. A path is blocked if it contains a chain or fork whose middle node is in Z, or a collider whose middle node (and all descendants) is not in Z. d-Separation implies conditional independence – these are testable predictions of the model. If data violates an implied independence, the model must be revised.

title: Chapter 2: Qualitative Causal Models
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter builds and analyzes the full MNH qualitative DAG in R, using dagitty for DAG specification and d-separation testing, and ggdag for visualization.

Define the Full MNH DAG
We specify the comprehensive 10-node qualitative DAG with positional coordinates for layout, representing the MNH program’s theory of change from budget allocation through mediating variables to mortality outcomes.

library(dagitty)
library(ggdag)
library(ggplot2)

# Define the comprehensive MNH qualitative DAG
mnh_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Budget          [pos="0,1"]
  Workforce       [pos="1.5,0"]
  Training        [pos="1.5,1"]
  Equipment       [pos="1.5,2"]
  Referral_System [pos="1.5,3"]
  Coverage        [pos="3,3"]
  Quality         [pos="3,1"]
  Baseline_Burden [pos="4,3"]
  NMR             [pos="4.5,0.5"]
  MMR             [pos="4.5,1.5"]

  Budget -> Workforce
  Budget -> Training
  Budget -> Equipment
  Budget -> Referral_System
  Training -> Quality
  Workforce -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Referral_System -> Coverage
  Coverage -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
  Quality -> MMR
  Baseline_Burden -> NMR
  Baseline_Burden -> MMR
}')

# Verify it is a valid DAG (acyclic)
isAcyclic(mnh_dag)



Visualize the DAG Color-Coded by Role
We merge node role metadata into the tidy DAG data to enable color-coding by function: decision nodes, mediators, contextual variables, and outcome objectives.

# Create a tidy dag object with exposure and outcome
tidy_dag <- tidy_dagitty(mnh_dag)

# Define node roles for coloring
node_roles <- data.frame(
  name = c("Budget", "Workforce", "Training", "Equipment",
           "Referral_System", "Coverage", "Quality",
           "Baseline_Burden", "NMR", "MMR"),
  role = c("Decision", "Mediator", "Mediator", "Mediator",
           "Mediator", "Mediator", "Mediator",
           "Context", "Outcome", "Outcome")
)

# Merge roles into the dag data
dag_data <- left_join(as.data.frame(tidy_dag), node_roles,
                      by = "name")

ggdag(mnh_dag) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Program: Qualitative Causal Model",
       subtitle = "10-node DAG for MNH investment decisions") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#005CB9", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666", size = 12))



Test d-Separation
The dseparated() function lets us verify the conditional independence predictions implied by our DAG. Each test checks whether information flows between two variables given a conditioning set.

# Test 1: Is Training d-separated from NMR given Quality?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Training", "NMR", "Quality")
# Expected: TRUE (chain is blocked)

# Test 2: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment? (unconditional)
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", c())
# Expected: FALSE (common parent Budget creates fork)

# Test 3: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment given Budget?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", "Budget")
# Expected: TRUE (conditioning on common cause blocks fork)

# Test 4: Is Workforce d-separated from Equipment given Quality?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Workforce", "Equipment", "Quality")
# Expected: FALSE (conditioning on collider opens path!)

# Test 5: Is Training d-separated from Baseline_Burden?
dseparated(mnh_dag, "Training", "Baseline_Burden", c())
# Expected: TRUE (no connecting path)



Enumerate All Paths
The paths() function lists every path between two nodes in the DAG, showing both directed (causal) and non-directed (non-causal) paths. This is useful for understanding the full set of channels through which one variable can influence another.

# Find all paths from Budget to NMR
budget_nmr_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                          from = "Budget",
                          to = "NMR")
print(budget_nmr_paths)

# Find all paths from Budget to MMR
budget_mmr_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                          from = "Budget",
                          to = "MMR")
print(budget_mmr_paths)

# Find paths between Workforce and Equipment (through collider)
workforce_equip_paths <- paths(mnh_dag,
                               from = "Workforce",
                               to = "Equipment")
print(workforce_equip_paths)



Implied Conditional Independencies
The impliedConditionalIndependencies() function extracts every testable prediction from the DAG. These are the conditional independencies that must hold in the data if the model is correctly specified.

# List all conditional independencies implied by the DAG
implied_cis <- impliedConditionalIndependencies(mnh_dag)
print(implied_cis)

# This tells us every testable prediction of our model.
# For example, if our model is correct, we should observe:
#   - Training _||_ Equipment | Budget
#   - Workforce _||_ Referral_System | Budget
#   - Training _||_ Baseline_Burden
#   - NMR _||_ MMR | Quality, Baseline_Burden
#
# If DHIS2 data violates any of these, we need to revise the DAG.

# Count total number of implied independencies
cat("Total implied conditional independencies:", length(implied_cis), "\n")

title: Chapter 2: Qualitative Causal Models
text:
Key Takeaways

Qualitative causal models are the foundation of rigorous analysis. A DAG encodes your causal beliefs explicitly using four node types and signed directed links.
The three triplet structures – chain, fork, and collider – govern all information flow. Chains and forks are blocked by conditioning; colliders are opened by conditioning.
d-Separation translates graph structure into testable conditional independence predictions.
For MNH programs: the qualitative DAG reveals that People, Products, and Systems investments are complements flowing through Quality of Care – and that conditioning on Quality creates collider bias between investment pillars.

title: Inference and Intervention
text:
This course adapts the frameworks from Inference and Intervention: Causal Models for Business Analysis (Ryall & Bramson, 2014) for professionals working in maternal and newborn health (MNH) programs. Across 10 chapters, you will build a complete toolkit for causal reasoning — from constructing qualitative models to making data-driven resource allocation decisions.

The central message: Causal models are not just academic abstractions — they are practical tools for answering the questions that matter most in global health: Where should we invest? What should we do? How do we know it will work?

title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Causal Analysis
text:
Key Concepts

Situational Assessment
Using evidence to reason about the current state of a system — determining which variables are in which states, and why. This is the “What is happening?” question every manager faces.


Managerial Intervention
A decision about which objects to control or change — the choice of what to act on, before deciding how to act. Resources are always limited, so every intervention involves trade-offs.


The Three Causal Thinking Traps








Trap
What Happens
MNH Example




Confounding
Third variable drives both X and Y
Disease burden drives both worker deployment and mortality


Reverse Causation
Y causes X, not X causes Y
Mortality attracts funding, not vice versa


Selection Bias
Non-random sample distorts relationship
Only high-capacity hospitals report data





Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
A causal diagram with no cycles — you cannot follow arrows from any node back to itself. An arrow from A to B means that changing A can change B, but changing B does not change A. This is the mathematical foundation of causal modeling.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Causal Analysis
text:
Lecture Slides




Open slides full-screen →

title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Causal Analysis
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter introduces the core R packages for causal analysis: dagitty for DAG specification and analysis, ggdag for visualization, and ggplot2 for general plotting.

Setting Up the Environment

# Install packages (run once)
# install.packages(c("dagitty", "ggdag", "ggplot2", "bnlearn"))

# Load packages
library(dagitty)    # DAG specification and analysis
library(ggdag)      # DAG visualization with ggplot2
library(ggplot2)    # General plotting



A Simple MNH Causal Model

# Define a simple 3-node DAG
mnh_simple <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,0"]
  Coverage [pos="1,0"]
  NMR [pos="2,0"]

  Investment -> Coverage
  Coverage -> NMR
}')

# Visualize
ggdag(mnh_simple) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Simple MNH Causal Model",
       subtitle = "Investment → Coverage → Neonatal Mortality Rate")



A Confounded Model

# Hospital delivery and mortality: confounded by complication severity
confounded_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Complication_Severity [pos="1,0"]
  Hospital_Delivery [pos="0,1"]
  Neonatal_Mortality [pos="2,1"]

  Complication_Severity -> Hospital_Delivery
  Complication_Severity -> Neonatal_Mortality
  Hospital_Delivery -> Neonatal_Mortality
}')

# Visualize with exposure and outcome
ggdag_status(confounded_dag,
             exposure = "Hospital_Delivery",
             outcome = "Neonatal_Mortality") +
  theme_dag() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("exposure" = "#83BD00",
                                "outcome" = "#E24A3F",
                                "other" = "#F8A623")) +
  labs(title = "Confounding in Hospital Delivery Data")



Finding Adjustment Sets

# What must we control for to estimate the causal effect
# of hospital delivery on mortality?
adjustmentSets(confounded_dag,
               exposure = "Hospital_Delivery",
               outcome = "Neonatal_Mortality")
# Returns: { Complication_Severity }

To estimate the true causal effect of hospital delivery on neonatal mortality, we must control for complication severity. Without this adjustment, the correlation between hospital delivery and mortality is biased by confounding.


A Richer MNH Model

# MNH initiative theory of change
mnh_toc <- dagitty('dag {
  Budget [pos="0,1"]
  Workforce [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,1"]
  Data_Systems [pos="1,2"]
  Quality [pos="2,1"]
  NMR [pos="3,1"]
  Baseline_Burden [pos="1.5,2.5"]

  Budget -> Workforce
  Budget -> Equipment
  Budget -> Data_Systems
  Workforce -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Data_Systems -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
  Baseline_Burden -> NMR
  Baseline_Burden -> Budget
}')

# Identify the adjustment set for Budget → NMR
adjustmentSets(mnh_toc,
               exposure = "Budget",
               outcome = "NMR")

# Visualize
ggdag(mnh_toc) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Initiative: Theory of Change as a DAG")

title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Causal Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways

Causal modeling serves two purposes: situational assessment (What is happening?) and managerial intervention (What should I do?).
Correlation ≠ causation. Confounding, reverse causation, and selection bias all create misleading associations.
A causal model makes your assumptions explicit, testable, and actionable — essential for allocating large-scale program funding effectively.

title: Chapter 3: The MNH Diagnostic Case Study
text:
Key Concepts

Diagnostic Causal Modeling
An iterative cycle of hypothesis, stakeholder interview, model revision, deeper questions, and refinement. The process begins with the simplest possible model and adds complexity only as interviews reveal new variables and mechanisms. At each stage, the model tells you what questions to ask next – this is the power of structured causal thinking.


The Four Diagnostic Principles
The diagnostic approach follows four key principles. First, focus on significant drivers – not all variables matter equally, so identify those with the largest causal effects on the objective. Second, seek sources of common problems – look for common causes that explain multiple symptoms, as these are high-leverage intervention points. Third, ask specific questions – use the model to generate targeted hypotheses rather than open-ended inquiries. Fourth, bring it all together – the final model should tell a coherent causal story linking decisions to outcomes.


Supply Side vs. Demand Side
A complete diagnostic model must capture both supply-side and demand-side factors. On the supply side, investment flows through channels like training, equipment, and staffing but can be undermined by transmission failures such as staff attrition, equipment breakdown, and referral gaps. On the demand side, geographic access, health-seeking behavior, and financial barriers determine whether communities actually benefit from facility-level improvements. Both sides must work together to reduce mortality.


Root Nodes as Leverage Points
Root nodes are variables without any parents in the causal model – they are ultimate causes. Intervening on root nodes has the broadest downstream effects. In the MNH diagnostic case, staff retention and equipment maintenance emerged as critical root-level bottlenecks that the initial investment plan had overlooked. The model reveals that investing in the right inputs (training, equipment) is necessary but not sufficient when transmission mechanisms are broken.

title: Chapter 3: The MNH Diagnostic Case Study
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter demonstrates how to formalize each round of the diagnostic interview process in R, building progressively more complex DAGs with dagitty and ggdag.

Round 1: Simple Supply-Side Model
After the initial interviews with the ministry of health, we decompose the investment-to-outcome chain into its supply-side components: training, equipment, staffing, competency, and utilization.

library(dagitty)
library(ggdag)
library(ggplot2)

# Round 1: Simple supply-side model
round1 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,1"]
  Staffing [pos="1,2"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,1.5"]
  Coverage [pos="3,1"]
  NMR [pos="4,1"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Staffing -> Utilization
  Competency -> Coverage
  Utilization -> Coverage
  Coverage -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round1) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 1: Supply-Side Model (7 nodes)")



Round 2: Adding Transmission Failures
Facility director interviews revealed that staff retention, equipment maintenance, and referral system gaps are critical missing variables. These common causes explain why inputs were delivered but effective use remains low.

# Round 2: Add retention, maintenance, referral
round2 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing [pos="1,1"]
  Retention [pos="1.5,0.5"]
  Maintenance [pos="1.5,2.5"]
  Referral [pos="1.5,3"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,2"]
  Coverage [pos="3,1"]
  NMR [pos="4,1"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Investment -> Referral
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Retention -> Competency
  Retention -> Utilization
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Maintenance -> Utilization
  Competency -> Coverage
  Utilization -> Coverage
  Referral -> Coverage
  Coverage -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round2) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 2: Adding Transmission Failures (10 nodes)")



Round 3: Complete Model with Demand Side
Community health worker interviews added demand-side variables: geographic access, health-seeking behavior, and financial barriers. These factors determine whether communities benefit from improved facility quality.

# Round 3: Add demand-side variables
round3 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1.5"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing [pos="1,1"]
  Retention [pos="1.5,0.5"]
  Maintenance [pos="1.5,2.5"]
  Referral [pos="1.5,3.5"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,2"]
  Access [pos="2,3.5"]
  Behavior [pos="2,4.5"]
  Fin_Barriers [pos="2,5.5"]
  Facility_Births [pos="3,4"]
  Quality [pos="3,1.5"]
  NMR [pos="4,2.5"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Investment -> Referral
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Retention -> Competency
  Retention -> Utilization
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Maintenance -> Utilization
  Competency -> Quality
  Utilization -> Quality
  Referral -> Access
  Access -> Facility_Births
  Behavior -> Facility_Births
  Fin_Barriers -> Facility_Births
  Quality -> NMR
  Facility_Births -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round3) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 3: Complete Model (15 nodes)")



Identifying What to Measure
The final model’s structure tells us which variables must be measured and controlled for in any evaluation, what conditional independencies the model predicts, and which causal paths connect investment to mortality outcomes.

# What must we control for to estimate the causal effect
# of investment on NMR?
adjustmentSets(round3,
               exposure = "Investment",
               outcome = "NMR")

# What are the implied conditional independencies?
# These are testable predictions of the model
impliedConditionalIndependencies(round3)

# What are all paths from Investment to NMR?
paths(round3,
      from = "Investment",
      to = "NMR")

The adjustmentSets() function tells us which variables we must measure and control for in our evaluation. The impliedConditionalIndependencies() function generates testable predictions – if the data violates them, our model is wrong and needs revision.

title: Chapter 3: The MNH Diagnostic Case Study
text:
Key Takeaways

Build models iteratively. Start simple and add complexity as interviews reveal new variables and mechanisms.
Root nodes are leverage points. Staff retention and equipment maintenance emerged as critical bottlenecks that the initial investment plan overlooked.
The model guides the analysis. Each round’s model tells you what questions to ask next, what data to collect, and where the bottlenecks are.

title: Chapter 5: Situational Analysis
text:
Key Concepts

Situational Analysis
The process of observing outcomes and using a quantitative causal model to infer the most likely state of unobserved causes. Where Chapter 4 ran the model “forward” (given causes, predict effects), situational analysis runs the model “backward” (given effects, infer causes). This backward reasoning – from children to parents – is the heart of diagnostic decision-making.


Marginal Probability
The probability of a variable computed by summing out (marginalizing over) all other variables in the joint distribution. It tells us the overall likelihood of an event, averaging across all possible parent combinations weighted by how likely each is. For example, the marginal P(NMR=High) answers: “What is the overall probability that a randomly selected district has high neonatal mortality?” – before any district-specific evidence.


Explaining Away
The most counterintuitive phenomenon in causal reasoning. In a converging structure (A -> B <- C), when two independent causes compete to explain the same effect, confirming one cause makes the other less likely and disconfirming one makes the other more likely. For example, if a district has good workforce quality despite low staffing, equipment availability must be high – equipment “explains away” what staffing cannot. This only happens when we condition on the common effect (the collider).


Information Flow Across Structures
The three triplet structures determine how evidence propagates through the model. In serial chains (A -> B -> C), observing B blocks information flow from A to C. In diverging forks (A <- B -> C), observing the common cause B blocks the spurious correlation between A and C. In converging colliders (A -> B <- C), the path is naturally blocked but observing B opens it, creating dependence between the previously independent causes. These rules scale to full graphs via d-separation.

title: Chapter 5: Situational Analysis
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter implements Bayesian inference in R using bnlearn and gRain, demonstrating how to build a network with CPTs, query it with evidence, compare prior vs. posterior beliefs, and run a full district diagnostic workflow.

Build a Bayesian Network with CPTs
We construct a 5-node Bayesian network (Staffing, Equipment, Training, Quality, NMR) with CPTs calibrated to MNH program data. The Quality node has an 8-row CPT reflecting all combinations of its three parents.

library(bnlearn)
library(gRain)

# Define the DAG structure
dag <- model2network("[Staffing][Equipment][Training][Quality|Staffing:Equipment:Training][NMR|Quality]")

# Define CPTs
cpt_s <- matrix(c(0.40, 0.60), ncol = 2,
                dimnames = list(NULL, c("Adequate", "Low")))

cpt_e <- matrix(c(0.35, 0.65), ncol = 2,
                dimnames = list(NULL, c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_t <- matrix(c(0.50, 0.50), ncol = 2,
                dimnames = list(NULL, c("Completed", "Not_Completed")))

# Quality CPT: P(Quality | Staffing, Equipment, Training)
cpt_q <- array(
  c(0.95, 0.05,  # S=Adeq, E=Avail, T=Comp
    0.80, 0.20,  # S=Adeq, E=Avail, T=Not
    0.65, 0.35,  # S=Adeq, E=Unavail, T=Comp
    0.45, 0.55,  # S=Adeq, E=Unavail, T=Not
    0.55, 0.45,  # S=Low, E=Avail, T=Comp
    0.35, 0.65,  # S=Low, E=Avail, T=Not
    0.20, 0.80,  # S=Low, E=Unavail, T=Comp
    0.08, 0.92), # S=Low, E=Unavail, T=Not
  dim = c(2, 2, 2, 2),
  dimnames = list(Quality = c("Good", "Poor"),
                  Staffing = c("Adequate", "Low"),
                  Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable"),
                  Training = c("Completed", "Not_Completed")))

# NMR CPT: P(NMR | Quality)
cpt_nmr <- matrix(c(0.85, 0.15, 0.30, 0.70), ncol = 2,
                  dimnames = list(NMR = c("Low", "High"),
                                  Quality = c("Good", "Poor")))

# Fit the BN with custom CPTs
bn_fit <- custom.fit(dag, list(
  Staffing = cpt_s, Equipment = cpt_e, Training = cpt_t,
  Quality = cpt_q, NMR = cpt_nmr))



Query with Evidence
The cpquery() function uses likelihood weighting to estimate conditional probabilities. We demonstrate marginal queries (no evidence), forward queries (evidence on root nodes), and backward queries (evidence on outcome nodes – the core of situational analysis).

# --- Marginal probability (no evidence) ---
cpquery(bn_fit,
        event = (NMR == "High"),
        evidence = TRUE,
        n = 1e6)
# ≈ 0.455

# --- Posterior with evidence: NMR is High ---
# What is P(Staffing = Low | NMR = High)?
cpquery(bn_fit,
        event = (Staffing == "Low"),
        evidence = (NMR == "High"),
        n = 1e6)
# ≈ 0.71

# --- Evidence on a root node ---
# What is P(NMR = High | Staffing = Low)?
cpquery(bn_fit,
        event = (NMR == "High"),
        evidence = (Staffing == "Low"),
        n = 1e6)
# ≈ 0.55

# --- Multiple pieces of evidence ---
# P(NMR = High | Staffing = Low, Equipment = Unavailable)
cpquery(bn_fit,
        event = (NMR == "High"),
        evidence = (Staffing == "Low" & Equipment == "Unavailable"),
        n = 1e6)
# ≈ 0.63



Compare Posterior vs. Prior
This block computes posterior probabilities for all root nodes given an NMR observation, allowing us to see which causes shift the most and therefore carry the strongest diagnostic signal.

# Function to compute posteriors for all root nodes given NMR evidence
compute_posteriors <- function(bn, nmr_state, n_samples = 1e6) {
  data.frame(
    Variable = c("Staffing=Low", "Equipment=Unavail", "Training=Not_Comp"),
    Prior = c(0.60, 0.65, 0.50),
    Posterior = c(
      cpquery(bn, (Staffing == "Low"), (NMR == nmr_state), n = n_samples),
      cpquery(bn, (Equipment == "Unavailable"), (NMR == nmr_state), n = n_samples),
      cpquery(bn, (Training == "Not_Completed"), (NMR == nmr_state), n = n_samples)
    )
  )
}

# Posteriors given NMR=High (District Mwanga)
post_high <- compute_posteriors(bn_fit, "High")
post_high$Shift <- post_high$Posterior - post_high$Prior
print(post_high)

# Posteriors given NMR=Low (District Songea)
post_low <- compute_posteriors(bn_fit, "Low")
post_low$Shift <- post_low$Posterior - post_low$Prior
print(post_low)



Visualize Belief Updating
A grouped bar chart comparing prior and posterior probabilities makes the belief shifts visually clear. All “bad” states become more likely after observing high mortality.

library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)

# Combine prior and posterior into tidy format
viz_data <- data.frame(
  Variable = rep(c("Staffing\n(Low)", "Equipment\n(Unavail)", "Training\n(Not Comp)"), 2),
  Condition = rep(c("Prior", "Posterior\n(NMR=High)"), each = 3),
  Probability = c(
    0.60, 0.65, 0.50,          # Priors
    0.710, 0.738, 0.573         # Posteriors given NMR=High
  )
)
viz_data$Condition <- factor(viz_data$Condition,
                              levels = c("Prior", "Posterior\n(NMR=High)"))

ggplot(viz_data, aes(x = Variable, y = Probability, fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", Probability * 100)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.6), vjust = -0.3, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Prior" = "#00A1DF", "Posterior\n(NMR=High)" = "#E24A3F"),
                    name = "") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Belief Updating: Prior vs. Posterior Given NMR = High",
       subtitle = "All 'bad' states become more likely after observing high mortality",
       x = NULL, y = "Probability") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        plot.title = element_text(color = "#005CB9", face = "bold"))



Full District Diagnosis Workflow
This reusable function accepts a fitted Bayesian network and a list of evidence, then computes posterior probabilities for all root nodes and ranks them by the magnitude of their belief shift. This is the complete diagnostic workflow for a district assessment.

# ---- Full diagnosis workflow for a district ----

diagnose_district <- function(bn, evidence_list, n_samples = 1e6) {
  # Build evidence string
  ev_expr <- paste(names(evidence_list), "==",
                   paste0('"', evidence_list, '"'), collapse = " & ")

  # Query each root node
  results <- data.frame(
    Node = c("Staffing", "Equipment", "Training"),
    Bad_State = c("Low", "Unavailable", "Not_Completed"),
    Prior = c(0.60, 0.65, 0.50)
  )

  results$Posterior <- sapply(1:nrow(results), function(i) {
    cpquery(bn,
            event = eval(parse(text = paste0(results$Node[i], ' == "',
                                              results$Bad_State[i], '"'))),
            evidence = eval(parse(text = ev_expr)),
            n = n_samples)
  })

  results$Shift <- results$Posterior - results$Prior
  results$Priority <- rank(-abs(results$Shift))
  results[order(results$Priority), ]
}

# Diagnose District Mwanga (NMR=High)
cat("=== District Mwanga Diagnosis ===\n")
mwanga <- diagnose_district(bn_fit, list(NMR = "High"))
print(mwanga)

# Diagnose with additional evidence (NMR=High, Quality=Poor)
cat("\n=== With additional evidence: Quality=Poor ===\n")
mwanga_deep <- diagnose_district(bn_fit,
                                  list(NMR = "High", Quality = "Poor"))
print(mwanga_deep)

title: Chapter 5: Situational Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways

Marginal probabilities tell us baseline expectations. The marginal P(NMR=High) = 45.5% is the starting point before any district-specific evidence.
Evidence updates beliefs via Bayes’ rule. Observing NMR=High shifts all root causes toward their “bad” states – with staffing showing the largest shift.
The three structures determine information flow: serial and diverging chains are blocked by conditioning on the middle node; converging chains are activated by it.
Explaining away is the most counterintuitive phenomenon. In a collider, observing the common effect makes independent causes compete – confirming one reduces belief in the other.

title: Chapter 7: Decision Analysis & Value of Information
text:
Key Concepts

Influence Diagram
A causal model that includes at least one strategic option (decision) and one objective, in addition to the usual chance variables. Also called a decision network. Bayesian networks (Chapters 2–6) contain only chance nodes and support inference; influence diagrams add decision nodes (rectangles) and objective nodes (hexagons), enabling optimal decision analysis.


Expected Value (EV)
The probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes for a given decision: \(EV(\text{Decision}) = \sum_{\text{outcomes}} P(\text{outcome} \mid do(\text{Decision})) \times V(\text{outcome})\). Comparing the EV across alternative intervention strategies provides a principled basis for choosing the option that maximizes expected lives saved.


Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)
The maximum amount a decision-maker should pay to learn the true state of an uncertain variable before deciding. Computed as \(\text{EVPI} = EV(\text{with perfect info}) - EV(\text{without info})\). EVPI sets the upper bound on how much a pilot program, survey, or study is worth.


The do-Operator and Graph Surgery
\(do(X = x)\) is an intervention that sets variable X to value x regardless of X’s parents in the causal model. Graph surgery deletes all arrows pointing into X, isolating the true causal effect from confounded associations. Observing that a facility has CPAP (\(P(Y \mid X)\)) is fundamentally different from giving a facility CPAP (\(P(Y \mid do(X))\)).


Sensitivity Analysis
Varying uncertain parameters to determine which ones matter most for the decision. In MNH, the probability of government co-financing often determines whether a workforce-heavy or equipment-heavy strategy is optimal. A sensitivity analysis identifies the crossover point where the optimal strategy changes.

title: Chapter 7: Decision Analysis & Value of Information
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter implements decision analysis in R, covering expected value computation, EVPI calculation, sensitivity analysis visualization, and graph surgery using Bayesian networks.

Expected Value Calculation
We define a three-strategy decision problem for MNH intervention deployment and compute the expected lives saved for each option.

# Define the decision problem
strategies <- data.frame(
  strategy = rep(c("CPAP Only", "Workforce", "Split"), each = 2),
  gov_support = rep(c("High", "Low"), 3),
  probability = rep(c(0.40, 0.60), 3),
  lives_saved = c(2800, 1200,   # CPAP
                  3600, 1000,    # Workforce
                  3100, 1400)    # Split
)

# Compute expected value per strategy
library(dplyr)
ev_table <- strategies %>%
  group_by(strategy) %>%
  summarise(
    expected_lives = sum(probability * lives_saved),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(expected_lives))

ev_table
# Split: 2080, Workforce: 2040, CPAP: 1840



EVPI Calculation
EVPI tells us the maximum value of learning the true state of government co-financing before committing funds. It is computed as the difference between the expected value with perfect information and the expected value of the best uninformed strategy.

# EVPI = E[max value per scenario] - max E[value]

# Best decision in each scenario
best_per_scenario <- strategies %>%
  group_by(gov_support) %>%
  slice_max(lives_saved) %>%
  ungroup()

# Expected value with perfect information
ev_perfect <- sum(best_per_scenario$probability *
                  best_per_scenario$lives_saved)

# Expected value without information (best overall strategy)
ev_no_info <- max(ev_table$expected_lives)

# EVPI
evpi <- ev_perfect - ev_no_info
cat("EV with perfect info:", ev_perfect, "\n")
cat("EV without info:", ev_no_info, "\n")
cat("EVPI:", evpi, "lives\n")
# EVPI tells us the maximum value of learning government intentions



Sensitivity Analysis Visualization
This plot shows how the expected lives saved for each strategy varies as the probability of high government co-financing changes. The crossover points reveal when the optimal strategy shifts.

library(ggplot2)

# Vary P(High co-financing) from 0 to 1
p_seq <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
sensitivity <- data.frame(
  p = rep(p_seq, 3),
  strategy = rep(c("CPAP Only", "Workforce", "Split"),
                 each = length(p_seq)),
  ev = c(1200 + 1600 * p_seq,   # CPAP
         1000 + 2600 * p_seq,    # Workforce
         1400 + 1700 * p_seq)    # Split
)

ggplot(sensitivity, aes(x = p, y = ev, color = strategy)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.40, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "P(High Government Co-financing)",
       y = "Expected Lives Saved",
       title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Strategy Choice",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = current estimate (p = 0.40)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)



Graph Surgery with bnlearn
Graph surgery implements the do-operator by removing incoming arrows to the intervention variable. The original model includes confounding via Resources; the intervened model isolates the true causal effect of CPAP on NMR.

library(bnlearn)

# Original model (with confounding)
original <- model2network(
  "[Resources][CPAP|Resources][NMR|CPAP:Resources]"
)

# After graph surgery: do(CPAP) — remove Resources -> CPAP
intervened <- model2network(
  "[Resources][CPAP][NMR|CPAP:Resources]"
)

# The key difference:
# In 'original': P(NMR | CPAP=Yes) includes confounding via Resources
# In 'intervened': P(NMR | CPAP=Yes) is the true causal effect

# With fitted CPTs, you would query:
# cpquery(fit_original, (NMR == "Low"), (CPAP == "Yes"))
# cpquery(fit_intervened, (NMR == "Low"), (CPAP == "Yes"))
# The second query gives the causal effect



Tornado Diagram
A tornado diagram shows which uncertain parameter has the largest swing effect on the expected outcome of the Split strategy. Parameters with the widest bars deserve the most attention in data collection and negotiation.

# Sensitivity of Split strategy to each parameter
params <- data.frame(
  parameter = c("P(Gov High)", "Lives if High",
                "Lives if Low", "Cost ($M)"),
  low_ev  = c(1840, 1960, 1960, 2080),
  base_ev = c(2080, 2080, 2080, 2080),
  high_ev = c(2320, 2200, 2200, 2080)
)

params$swing <- params$high_ev - params$low_ev
params <- params[order(params$swing, decreasing = TRUE), ]
params$parameter <- factor(params$parameter,
                           levels = rev(params$parameter))

ggplot(params, aes(y = parameter)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = low_ev, xend = high_ev,
                   yend = parameter),
               size = 8, color = "#005CB9", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2080, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Expected Lives Saved", y = "",
       title = "Tornado Diagram: Sensitivity of Split Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: Chapter 9: Game Theory & Strategic Interactions
text:
Key Concepts

Nash Equilibrium
A strategy profile where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy, holding all other players’ strategies fixed. In MNH contexts, Nash equilibria often reveal free-riding behavior: the donor funds generously and the government reduces its own co-financing because individual incentives push toward a suboptimal collective outcome.


Extensive-Form Games and Backward Induction
A game represented as a tree showing the sequence of moves, information available at each decision point, and payoffs at terminal nodes. Backward induction solves the game by starting at terminal nodes and working backward. The order of moves matters: when the donor commits first without conditions, the government can observe and free-ride.


Commitment Devices and Mechanism Design
Contract structures that change the payoff matrix so that cooperation becomes the dominant strategy. Milestone-based payments, bonding agreements, and conditional funding triggers are all commitment devices. They work by making free-riding costly and cooperation genuinely attractive for both sides. Credibility is essential: conditionalities only work if the donor would actually withhold funding when milestones are missed.


Causal-Form Games
A multi-agent influence diagram where each player has their own decision node and objective node, and the causal structure shows how decisions interact to produce outcomes. The causal form connects game theory to the DAG-based causal models of earlier chapters, making explicit why payoffs have their values and how decisions flow through intermediate variables to outcomes.


Strategy Tables
A complete enumeration of contingent plans specifying what a player will do at every decision point, contingent on what they observe. With 2 information sets and 2 actions each, a player has \(2^2 = 4\) possible strategies. Conditional strategies such as “Match” (cooperate if and only if the other player cooperates) are central to designing effective funding agreements.
