title: Chapter 9: Game Theory & Strategic Interactions
text:
R Code Workshop
This chapter implements game theory analysis in R, including Nash equilibrium computation, payoff matrix visualization, backward induction for extensive-form games, and simulation of repeated games with conditional strategies.

Representing and Solving a 2x2 Game
We define the Workforce Absorption Game as payoff matrices and implement a general Nash equilibrium finder using the best-response method.

# Define the Workforce Absorption Game (2-player, 2x3 reduced to 2x2)
# Payoffs: list(Donor, Government)

# Donor strategies: Full Funding, Reduced Funding (rows)
# Gov strategies: Full Absorb, No Absorb (columns, simplified)

donor_payoffs <- matrix(c(4500, 2200,   # Full Absorb column
                          500,  300),    # No Absorb column
                        nrow = 2, ncol = 2,
                        dimnames = list(
                          Donor = c("Full Funding", "Reduced"),
                          Gov = c("Full Absorb", "No Absorb")))

gov_payoffs <- matrix(c(600, 400,     # Full Absorb column
                        1000, 800),   # No Absorb column
                      nrow = 2, ncol = 2,
                      dimnames = list(
                        Donor = c("Full Funding", "Reduced"),
                        Gov = c("Full Absorb", "No Absorb")))

# Find Nash Equilibria by checking best responses
find_nash <- function(p1, p2) {
  nr <- nrow(p1); nc <- ncol(p1)
  nash <- list()
  for (i in 1:nr) {
    for (j in 1:nc) {
      # Is i a best response for Player 1 given Player 2 plays j?
      p1_br <- p1[i, j] == max(p1[, j])
      # Is j a best response for Player 2 given Player 1 plays i?
      p2_br <- p2[i, j] == max(p2[i, ])
      if (p1_br & p2_br) {
        nash <- c(nash, list(c(row = i, col = j)))
      }
    }
  }
  nash
}

ne <- find_nash(donor_payoffs, gov_payoffs)
cat("Nash Equilibria:\n")
for (eq in ne) {
  cat(sprintf("  (%s, %s) -> Donor: %d, Gov: %d\n",
              rownames(donor_payoffs)[eq[1]],
              colnames(donor_payoffs)[eq[2]],
              donor_payoffs[eq[1], eq[2]],
              gov_payoffs[eq[1], eq[2]]))
}
# Expected output: (Full Funding, No Absorb) -> Donor: 500, Gov: 1000



Payoff Matrix Heatmap
A heatmap visualization of the full 2x3 payoff matrix for the Workforce Absorption Game, with cell labels showing both players’ payoffs and color encoding the donor’s outcome.

library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)

# Build tidy data for both players
game_df <- expand.grid(
  Donor = c("Full Funding", "Reduced"),
  Gov = c("Full Absorb", "Partial Absorb", "No Absorb")
)
game_df$Donor_Payoff <- c(4500, 2200, 2800, 1500, 500, 300)
game_df$Gov_Payoff   <- c(600,  400,  900,  700,  1000, 800)
game_df$Total        <- game_df$Donor_Payoff + game_df$Gov_Payoff
game_df$Label <- paste0("D: ", game_df$Donor_Payoff,
                        "\nG: ", game_df$Gov_Payoff)

# Factor ordering
game_df$Donor <- factor(game_df$Donor,
                        levels = c("Full Funding", "Reduced"))
game_df$Gov   <- factor(game_df$Gov,
                        levels = c("Full Absorb", "Partial Absorb",
                                   "No Absorb"))

ggplot(game_df, aes(x = Gov, y = Donor, fill = Donor_Payoff)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Label), size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#E24A3F", mid = "#FED141",
                       high = "#83BD00", midpoint = 2000,
                       name = "Donor\nPayoff") +
  labs(title = "Workforce Absorption Game: Payoff Matrix",
       x = "Government Strategy", y = "Donor Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(color = "#005CB9", face = "bold"))



Extensive Form – Backward Induction
We solve the sequential version of the game where the donor moves first and the government observes the donor’s choice before responding. Backward induction reveals the subgame perfect equilibrium.

# Extensive form: Donor moves first, Government observes and responds
# Define terminal payoffs: (Donor, Gov)
terminal <- list(
  "Full->FullAbsorb"    = c(D = 4500, G = 600),
  "Full->PartialAbsorb" = c(D = 2800, G = 900),
  "Full->NoAbsorb"      = c(D = 500,  G = 1000),
  "Reduced->FullAbsorb"    = c(D = 2200, G = 400),
  "Reduced->PartialAbsorb" = c(D = 1500, G = 700),
  "Reduced->NoAbsorb"      = c(D = 300,  G = 800)
)

# Step 1: Government's best response at each information set
gov_after_full <- terminal[grep("^Full->", names(terminal))]
gov_br_full <- names(which.max(sapply(gov_after_full, `[`, "G")))
cat("Gov best response after Full Funding:", gov_br_full, "\n")
cat("  Payoffs:", gov_after_full[[gov_br_full]], "\n\n")

gov_after_reduced <- terminal[grep("^Reduced->", names(terminal))]
gov_br_reduced <- names(which.max(sapply(gov_after_reduced, `[`, "G")))
cat("Gov best response after Reduced:", gov_br_reduced, "\n")
cat("  Payoffs:", gov_after_reduced[[gov_br_reduced]], "\n\n")

# Step 2: Donor anticipates and chooses
donor_full    <- gov_after_full[[gov_br_full]]["D"]
donor_reduced <- gov_after_reduced[[gov_br_reduced]]["D"]
cat("Donor payoff if Full:", donor_full, "\n")
cat("Donor payoff if Reduced:", donor_reduced, "\n")
cat("Donor optimal choice:",
    ifelse(donor_full >= donor_reduced, "Full Funding", "Reduced"), "\n")

# SPE: (Full Funding, Gov always plays No Absorb)
# Donor gets 500 lives — motivating the need for conditionality



Simulating a Repeated Game
A repeated-game simulation compares unconditional funding (always fund high) versus a tit-for-tat conditional strategy. The tit-for-tat approach induces government cooperation over time by punishing free-riding with reduced funding in the following round.

library(ggplot2)

# Simulate repeated interaction with tit-for-tat strategy
# Donor uses conditional strategy: fund high if Gov cooperated last round
set.seed(42)
n_rounds <- 20

# Strategy profiles
simulate_repeated <- function(donor_strategy, n = 20) {
  donor_action <- character(n)
  gov_action   <- character(n)
  donor_payoff <- numeric(n)
  gov_payoff   <- numeric(n)

  # Payoffs per round (lives saved in hundreds)
  payoff_matrix <- list(
    "High_High" = c(D = 24, G = 7),
    "High_Low"  = c(D = 5,  G = 10),
    "Low_High"  = c(D = 18, G = 5),
    "Low_Low"   = c(D = 8,  G = 6)
  )

  for (t in 1:n) {
    # Donor strategy
    if (donor_strategy == "unconditional") {
      donor_action[t] <- "High"
    } else if (donor_strategy == "tit_for_tat") {
      donor_action[t] <- if (t == 1) "High"
                          else if (gov_action[t-1] == "High") "High"
                          else "Low"
    }
    # Government: rational, but learns from consequences
    # Starts by free-riding, switches to cooperate if punished
    if (t <= 3) {
      gov_action[t] <- "Low"  # Test the donor
    } else if (donor_strategy == "tit_for_tat" &
               donor_action[t] == "Low") {
      gov_action[t] <- "High"  # Cooperate after punishment
    } else {
      gov_action[t] <- "Low"   # Free-ride if no punishment
    }

    key <- paste0(donor_action[t], "_", gov_action[t])
    donor_payoff[t] <- payoff_matrix[[key]]["D"]
    gov_payoff[t]   <- payoff_matrix[[key]]["G"]
  }

  data.frame(round = 1:n, donor_action, gov_action,
             donor_payoff, gov_payoff)
}

# Compare unconditional vs tit-for-tat
uncond <- simulate_repeated("unconditional")
uncond$strategy <- "Unconditional"
tft <- simulate_repeated("tit_for_tat")
tft$strategy <- "Tit-for-Tat"
results <- rbind(uncond, tft)

ggplot(results, aes(x = round, y = cumsum(donor_payoff),
                    color = strategy)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = gov_action), size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E24A3F", "#83BD00")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(High = 16, Low = 4),
                     name = "Gov Action") +
  labs(title = "Repeated Game: Cumulative Donor Payoff",
       subtitle = "Tit-for-tat induces Government cooperation over time",
       x = "Funding Round", y = "Cumulative Lives Saved (hundreds)",
       color = "Donor Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "#005CB9", face = "bold"))

title: Chapter 9: Game Theory & Strategic Interactions
text:
Key Takeaways

Game theory models strategic interaction among multiple agents – essential when governments, donors, and partners all make interdependent choices.
Nash equilibrium reveals the stable outcome when each agent optimizes individually. In MNH, this often means free-riding unless contracts are designed carefully.
Extensive-form analysis and backward induction show how the sequence of moves matters. Conditional commitments (milestone payments) transform the game’s equilibrium.
Strategy tables and the causal form connect game theory to the influence diagrams of Chapters 7–8, providing a unified framework for multi-agent decision problems.

Learning causal structure from MNH datasets
